architecture.summary: BiPRM augments standard Process Reward Models with a parallel right-to-left (R2L) evaluation stream alongside the conventional left-to-right (L2R) flow. The R2L stream is implemented via prompt reversal of the reasoning trajectory with no architectural changes to the underlying LLM. A lightweight gating mechanism (MLP) computes position-dependent weights to adaptively fuse reward scores from both streams, producing a final bidirectional reward per step. The trajectory-level reward applies a reduction operation (minimum) over step-wise scores.
trained_components: gating mechanism (MLP for dynamic weight computation)

claims.dataset: GSM-Plus and MATH500 (54 configurations across 2 benchmarks, 3 backbones, 3 objectives, 3 sampling policies)
claims.statement: BiPRM achieves an average relative gain of 10.6% over 54 solution-level configurations compared to unidirectional L2R baseline
BiPRM achieves 37.7% relative improvement in step-level error detection F1 score compared to L2R baseline
BiPRM introduces only 0.3% parameter increase for the gating module compared to L2R-PRM baseline
Parallel execution of bidirectional streams incurs approximately 5% wall-clock inference time latency increase compared to unidirectional L2R baseline
On Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B backbone, BiPRM achieves average F1 score of 30.2 on ProcessBench step-level error detection, compared to L2R baseline of 24.6

BiPRM dynamically learns to rely more on the R2L stream in early stages of reasoning (0%-30%) and progressively shifts focus to the L2R stream in later stages (70%-100%), as evidenced by monotonically increasing gating weights

BiPRM consistently maintains lower mean absolute error (MAE) throughout the entire reasoning trajectory compared to unidirectional L2R and R2L baselines, particularly suppressing error spikes in the transition zone around the average error onset (52.2% normalized progress)

Bidirectional evaluation mechanism necessitates processing the input sequence twice, leading to a two-fold increase in theoretical floating-point operations (FLOPs) compared to unidirectional baselines
